cards.title: Trust as Infrastructure
cards.description: Good technology can't be separated from the relationships that create it. We don't finish understanding how you work and then start building. **The building is part of the understanding**. The small fixes, the documentation, the slow learning alongside your team. We've found that when {_trust and technology develop together_}, what emerges tends to fit how you actually operate.

cards.title: Community Ownership
cards.description: What we build together draws on your team's knowledge and the communities you serve. **Tools shaped by community expertise should stay with the people who shaped them.** To use, change, and share. Open licensing is a structural choice we make from the start. It shapes our contracts, our tooling decisions, and what gets released.

cards.title: Data Sovereignty
cards.description: The data your organization holds isn't really organizational data. It's **data about the people you serve**. When that gets locked into vendor agreements or closed platforms, organizations lose something more than access. They lose the ability to **tell their own story, on their own terms**. __Indigenous-led data governance__, including {_OCAP principles_} here in Alberta, has shaped how we think about this. We're learning from that leadership and **aspiring to meet the standard it sets**. Where we encounter systems that don't support data portability, **working toward it is part of what we do together**. Not as an abstract principle, but practically. In how we structure databases, choose tools, and write contracts.

cards.title: Building Capacity, Not Dependency
cards.description: Technology should get **easier to manage over time**, not harder. We build with that in mind. We scope systems to what your team can realistically maintain and choose tools for longevity, not novelty. Over time, **your staff develop a working understanding of what's under the hood**, not just how to use it. The goal isn't an organization that needs us. It's one where **your people feel confident making changes**, asking the right questions, and knowing when something needs more care. We stay involved as long as it's useful. But what we build should work without us.
